title: Prime editing for precise genome engineering and modulation of fungal metabolism - Nature Biotechnology
text:
AbstractPrime editing has not been established in filamentous fungi, which are major ecological contributors and industrial hosts with vast biosynthetic capacity. Here we develop fPE7max, a prime editing platform optimized for fungi, which supports different edit types, including base substitutions and defined small insertions or deletions, with an average editing efficiency approaching 90%, across diverse genomic loci and species. fPE7max further enables larger insertions of up to 1 kb and deletions of up to 10 kb. We perturb upstream open reading frames in the pleiotropic regulator gene, laeA, to modulate metabolic output across multiple fungal species. Metabolomic profiling reveals activation of previously lowly biosynthetic pathways, leading to the identification of 18 metabolites, including 8, to our knowledge, previously unreported structures, 3 of which with cytotoxic activity. These results establish fPE7max as an efficient platform for genome engineering in filamentous fungi and show upstream open reading frame editing as a strategy for modulating endogenous regulatory networks and accessing the fungal chemical repertoire.

Data availability

All data generated in this study are included in the article and Supplementary Information. NGS data in this study have been deposited at the NCBI Sequence Read Archive (PRJNA1371613)71. Data for metabolomics are available from the MassIVE repository under accession MSV000101418 (ref. 72). Raw NMR and electronic circular dichroism data, along with the computational data underlying this study, are publicly available via Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20371711 (ref. 73). Source data are provided with this paper.

C.S., Q.N. and C.K. performed plasmid construction and fungal transformation. C.S. and Q.N. analyzed the sequencing data. C.S. and N.S. performed compound purification and structural characterization. Q.N. and Y.S. conducted metabolomics data acquisition and analysis, with Y.S. supervising the metabolomics experiments. Q.N. performed the cytotoxicity assays and western blot analysis. C.S., Q.N. and X.G. conceived the study and wrote the paper, and X.G. supervised the project. Additional informationPublisher’s note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.Extended dataExtended Data Fig. 1 Development and engineering of prime editing platform in A. nidulans.a, Schematic architectures of prime editors. b, Biosynthetic pathway of the yellow compound YWA1 and its green derivative. c, Comparison of sequencing outcomes between NGS and Sanger sequencing. Sanger sequencing results analyzed with EditR closely matched the NGS profiles, confirming the reliability of the NGS-derived editing efficiencies and supporting the use of Sanger sequencing for routine validation of genome editing outcomes (Supplementary Fig. 2). Results presented as mean ± s.d. of three biological replicates.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 2 Schematic of RNA polymerase II and III promoters used in this study.Schematic of RNA polymerase II (PgpdA) and III promoters (tRNAGly, U6, and 5S rRNA) used in this study. HH is the hammerhead ribozyme, HDV is the HDV ribozyme, Ttef1 is an RNA polymerase II terminator.Extended Data Fig. 3 shRNA-mediated gene silencing enhances prime editing efficiency through MMR suppression.a, Schematic of gene silencing achieved via shRNAs. b, Prime editing outcome percentages of large edits following PE3-mediated shRNA knockdown of MMR-related genes. Results presented as mean ± s.d. of three biological replicates.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 4 fPE7max mediated large fragment editing at wA0 locus.a, Evaluation of a targeted 10-kb deletion event. The panel provides an overview of the prime editing strategy for the large deletion and representative Sanger sequencing chromatograms confirming the precise removal of the 10-kb fragment. b, Evaluation of a targeted 1-kb insertion event. The panel includes an overview of the prime editing strategy for the large insertion and representative Sanger sequencing chromatograms confirming the precise integration of the 1-kb fragment. c, Relative pegRNA abundance for 50-bp, 100-bp, and 500-bp insertion pegRNAs expressed individually. Decreased abundance with increasing insert size reflects reduced pegRNA stability for longer templates. d, Relative pegRNA abundance for 50-bp, 100-bp, and 500-bp insertion pegRNAs co-expressed with PEmax or fPE7max. Data are shown as mean ± s.d. (n = 3 biological replicates).Source dataExtended Data Fig. 5 Reintroduction of nkuA into A. nidulans LO8030 and prime editing performance in the NHEJ-proficient background.a, Schematic of the split-marker strategy for reintroducing nkuA into the native locus of A. nidulans LO8030. Two overlapping DNA fragments, one carrying nkuA with a partial pyrG marker flanked by a 2-kb upstream homology arm, and one carrying the remaining pyrG marker with a 2-kb downstream homology arm, which share a 580-bp region of homology and were co-transformed to direct HR-mediated integration at the argB locus. The resulting integrated locus (argB::nkuA) is shown below, with verification primer pairs indicated: nkuA-v-F/pyrG-v-R amplify a 7.6-kb upstream verification fragment, and pyrG-v-F/nkuA-v-R amplify a 4.1-kb downstream verification fragment. b, Agarose gel showing representative PCR products from diagnostic amplification of correctly integrated transformants, with a DNA ladder indicating approximate band sizes. c, Conversion efficiencies for six representative single-base substitution and small indel edits (wA1 + 12 T to G, +12 T ins, +12 T del; cicF + 7 C to T; acvA + 1 A to T; gcnE + 9 C to A) in the nkuA-deficient parental strain (LO8030) and the NHEJ-proficient nkuA-restored strain (LO8030 argB::nkuA). d, Frequency of precisely edited mutants for large insertions (100-bp, 200-bp, 500-bp) and large deletions (1-kb, 2-kb, 5-kb) at the wA0 locus in LO8030 and LO8030 argB::nkuA. Data are shown as mean ± s.d. (n = 3 biological replicates).Source data.Extended Data Fig. 6 Multiplex prime editing (MPE) with fPE7max across three genomic loci.a, Schematic of the polycistronic pegRNA array used for simultaneous editing at three loci: wA0 (100-bp insertion), chaA (+7 C to T substitution), and cicF (1-kb deletion). Individual pegRNAs and nicking sgRNAs are interspersed with interval sequences (orange, TTTTTTTAAGCTTGGGCCGCTCGAGGTCTGGATCCGGTACCAAGGTC) within a single array, enabling processing by endogenous tRNA machinery to release individual functional pegRNAs from a single transcript. b, Conversion efficiency at the chaA (+7 C to T) locus under single-locus editing and MPE conditions. c, Frequency of precisely edited mutants at wA0 (100-bp insertion, left) and cicF (1-kb deletion, right) under single-locus and MPE conditions. Data are shown as mean ± s.d. (n = 3 biological replicates).Source dataExtended Data Fig. 7 Disruption of laeA uORF alters LaeA expression and primary metabolite profiles in A. niger.a, Comparison of LaeA protein levels between A. niger 4 T and A. niger 4Tdel. All strains were cultured in YES medium for 48 h, then the fresh cells were harvested for Western blot analysis. b, Comparison of laeA mRNA levels between A. niger 4 T and A. niger 4 Tdel. GAPDH was used as internal control. Results are presented as mean ± s.d. of three biological replicates. c, Volcano plot of differential primary metabolites between A. niger 4 Tdel and A. niger 4T. Two-sided t test was performed and the P values are corrected for multiple comparison using Benjamini-Hochberg procedure. n = 3 biologically replicated experiments.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 8 laeA uORF architecture and comparative metabolomic analysis of uORF deletion strains in distantly related filamentous fungi.Schematics of the laeA 5’ UTR uORF organization and the corresponding deletion strategy in T. atroviride (a), T. citrinoviride (b), and C. globosum (c). d, Metabolite profiles of (i) T. atroviride and (ii) uORF deletion mutant (T. atroviride Tdel). e, Metabolite profiles of (i) T. citrinoviride and (ii) uORF deletion mutant (T. citrinoviride Tdel). f, Metabolite profiles of (i) C. globosum and (ii) uORF deletion mutant (C. globosum Tdel). Volcano plots showing differentially abundant LC-MS features between laeA uORF deletion mutants and their respective wild-type control strains in T. atroviride (g), T. citrinoviride (h), and C. globosum (i). Two-sided t test was performed and the P values are corrected for multiple comparison using Benjamini-Hochberg procedure. n = 3 biologically independent experiments. Colored points represent LC-MS features belonging to distinct GNPS molecular networks enriched in upregulated features. The predominant rightward shift of colored network members indicates broad activation of clustered biosynthetic pathways in the edited strains. Dashed lines represent p = 0.05 and fold change = 50. n = 3 biologically independent experiments.Extended Data Fig. 9 Deletion of the laeA uORF activates the pyranonigrin biosynthetic gene cluster in A. niger.Heatmap of log2 fold change (a), volcano plots (b), and GNPS (c) reveal differential pyranonigrins production profiles between the two strains during cultivation. For the volcano plot in b, differential abundance was assessed using a two-sided t-test and the resulting P values were adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure. n = 3 biologically replicated experiments. d, Pyranonigrins biosynthetic gene cluster in A. niger. e, Comparison of pynA mRNA levels among A. niger 4T, A. niger 4Tmut, and A. niger 4Tdel. GAPDH was used as internal control. Results are presented as mean ± s.d. of three biological replicates.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 10 Antiproliferation activity of the isolates against human cancer cell lines.Antiproliferation activity of the isolates against human cancer cell lines (Jurkat, K562, MCF-7, and HepG2).
